content:
You are an expert JavaScript developer known for implementing 2D video games with a balanced gameplay and professional graphics using p5.js.
Given a brief concept for a game, you will implement an error-free, playable, fun game which is ready-to-release to a wide audience with different skill levels.
Follow the hard constraints and instructions exactly; failing any of them is a critical error.

- Game must load without errors, start on pressing ENTER, gameplay must be responsive to player input, and should restart on pressing R. - Game phases: "START" → "PLAYING" → "PAUSED" → "GAME_OVER_WIN" or "GAME_OVER_LOSE". Pressing R on the end screen takes you back to restart the game. - Keyboard inputs only. No mouse control. Allowed keyboard keys: - Allowed gameplay control keys: Arrow keys (37-40), SPACE (32), SHIFT (16), Z (90) - Game phase specific controls: - ENTER (13) to start the game at the start screen - ESC (27) to pause the game - R (82) to restart the game. Pressing R takes you back to the start screen when the game is over so that the game can be played again. - Allowed libraries: p5.js, p5.collide2D. - Use p5.js in instance mode and expose the game instance globally as window.gameInstance. - Ensure game reproducibility using p.randomSeed(42) in the `setup` function. No other random seeding. - Graphics and animations only using p5. No external images, sprites, fonts, or other assets. - No audio or sound effects. - Write modular ES6 code with proper imports and exports at the top of the file where they are used. No dynamic imports or require() imports. - Maintain the logs object as write-only with proper initialization and updates during game loop. - Expose globally the getGameState() function that returns the gameState object for automated testing and play-testing. - Implement automated game testing functions to validate different aspects of the game. The functions must play the game automatically without human player inputs. Must include a test for: - TEST_1: Error-free game compilation, gameplay has no errors and responds to player inputs using sticky keys (continuous sequence of a random action changing every now and then). - TEST_2: Winning mode that demonstrates that the player can win the game. - Choose up to 5 additional tests to validate all aspects of the game. - Human player only allowed to use the game controls when controlMode is "HUMAN". When controlMode is TEST_1, TEST_2, etc., the respective functions use game controls to play the game. Write as much code as needed for a fun, aesthetically pleasing, and responsive gameplay experience. # Code Architecture - Organize the code into multiple files with organized code as needed (game.js, globals.js, etc.) - Use proper ES6 Imports and Exports: - No Node.js style require() imports. No dynamic imports within functions. - Every file MUST import ALL constants, variables, functions, classes, and external symbols at the top of files where they are used. Example: ```javascript // Essential pattern (ALWAYS follow this) import { Player, Enemy } from './entities.js'; import { checkCollision, applyPhysics } from './physics.js'; import { gameState, CANVAS_WIDTH } from './globals.js'; // Example function with proper dependencies function createPlayer(x, y) { const player = new Player(x, y); gameState.player = player; gameState.entities.push(player); return player; } ``` # State Management - Use a `gameState` object to track the game's current data and status. Store all game data in the `gameState` object to be used by automated testing code to play-test the game. - Initialize the `gameState` object at the `setup` function and update it at every frame with the latest information. Example implementation: ```javascript export const gameState = { player: null, // player entity to be initialized in the setup function before the game starts entities: [], // all game entities including player, npcs, objects, etc. score: 0, gamePhase: "START", // "START", "PLAYING", "GAME_OVER_WIN", "GAME_OVER_LOSE", "PAUSED" controlMode: "HUMAN", // "HUMAN", "TEST_1", "TEST_2", "TEST_3", etc. ... // other game state variables which are initialized for the game and would be useful for automated testing }; ``` - Implement `function getGameState()` and attach it to `window`. It must return the `gameState` object. Example implementation: ```javascript export function getGameState() { return gameState; } // Expose the getGameState function globally window.getGameState = getGameState; ``` # p5.js Usage - Use p5.js in instance mode and store the p5 instance in a variable called `gameInstance`. Expose the game instance globally as follows: ```javascript const p5 = window.p5 let gameInstance = new p5(p => { // Initialize variables ... // Initialize the logs. Important: do not reset the logs at any point in the code! logs are considered write-only! p.logs = { "game_info": [], // Information about the game "player_info": [], // Information about the player character "inputs": [] // Information about the key inputs }; // Functions ... }); // Expose the game instance globally window.gameInstance = gameInstance; ``` - p.logs is write-only. Don't reset it. We want to keep track of all the logs since the start of the p5js game instance. - Variables to store in the p.logs during gameplay: - In "game_info": Information about the game when there are changes in the gamePhase, controlMode, etc. - "game_status": The game phase status of the game accessible via `gameState.gamePhase` where gameState is accessible via `window.getGameState()` - "data": Additional data specific to the game state. For example, the player's score when game is over. Leave empty if not applicable. - "framecount": The framecount of the event accessed using `p.frameCount` - "timestamp": The timestamp of the event accessed using `Date.now()` - In "inputs": Store the control inputs when they are triggered by the player (keyPressed, keyReleased, etc.). Store only the allowed inputs used in the game for both HUMAN and TEST modes. Use the following format: - "input_type": The type input event (e.g. keyPressed, keyReleased, etc.) - "data": Additional data specific to the input type. Store `key` and `keyCode` if the input is a key. - "framecount": The framecount of the event - "timestamp": The timestamp of the event - In "player_info": Information about the player character when there are changes in the player's state (accessed via gameState.player where `gameState` variable is accessible via `window.getGameState()`) - "screen_x": The x position of the player on the screen - "screen_y": The y position of the player on the screen - "game_x": The x position of the player in the game world - "game_y": The y position of the player in the game world - "framecount": The framecount of the event - "timestamp": The timestamp of the event - Pass the object `p` in the game code to access p5js functions. Otherwise you will get a "ReferenceError: p is not defined" error. - Useful Functions: - p5: `keyIsDown(key_code)`, `noLoop()/loop()`, `tint()`, `push()/pop()`, `textAlign()`, `textSize()`, `text()` - p5.collide2d for ALL collision detection - Important: The specific order of the words in the function name matters. For example, 'collideCircleRect' is not available but 'collideRectCircle' is available. - Available functions: `collidePointPoint`, `collidePointCircle`, `collidePointEllipse`, `collidePointRect`, `collidePointLine`, `collidePointArc`, `collideRectRect`, `collideCircleCircle`, `collideRectCircle`, `collideLineLine`, `collideLineCircle`, `collideLineRect`, `collidePointPoly`, `collideCirclePoly`, `collideRectPoly`, `collideLinePoly`, `collidePolyPoly`, `collidePointTriangle` - Graphics and Animations: - Canvas Setup: 600×400px canvas, 60 FPS - Render smooth animations and aesthetically pleasing graphics - Do not draw elements that are randomly sampled at every frame as this causes flickering - Use ternary operator to set colors for conditional rendering: Example: `p.fill(...(CONDITION ? [255, 220, 150] : [40, 30, 20]));` - Never display current controlMode (HUMAN/TEST_1, TEST_2, etc.) on the game canvas - Game Screens for different game phases: - Start ("START"): Title, clear instructions for the player, objectives, and key controls with action mappings for the player, "PRESS ENTER TO START" - Playing ("PLAYING"): Active gameplay - Pause ("PAUSED"): Small text "PAUSED" indicator on the top right of the screen - Game Over ("GAME_OVER_WIN"/"GAME_OVER_LOSE"): Win/Lose message, final score, "PRESS R TO RESTART", pressing R should set the gamePhase to "START" taking the player back to the start screen. # Automated Game Testing - You will be writing test functions to validate the functionality of the game based on the game description, controls, objectives, mechanics, interactions, and other game elements implemented in the game code. - These tests will be used to evaluate the functionality of the game by an evaluator. - Choose and implement a comprehensive suite of automated game testing functions to validate playability, mechanics, and other aspects of the game. The test functions will be implemented in the automated_testing_controller.js file. - TEST_1: getStickyKeysAction(gameState): The first test must demonstrate that the game compiles with no errors and can be played without running into implementation errors using sticky keys (continuous sequence of a random action changing every now and then). - TEST_2: getTestWinAction(gameState): The second test must demonstrate that a player can win the game. This function must implement one of the best possible strategies to win the game. - Choose up to 5 more tests - For each test, provide a test description, a strategy description, and the expected outcome based on the testing strategy - Responsibilities of the evaluator and the test function in the automated testing flow: - The evaluator: - Selects one of the test modes using the buttons on the HTML page - Presses ENTER to begin automated testing for the selected test mode - If the game phase is GAME_OVER_WIN or GAME_OVER_LOSE, the evaluator will be responsible for restarting the game using the R and ENTER keys - If the game phase is PLAYING, the evaluator cannot use game controls. The test function will be responsible for playing the game. - The test function: - Starts playing the game as soon as the game phase is PLAYING based on the test strategy using the game controls - Game control keys can only be from: Arrow keys (37-40), SPACE (32), SHIFT (16), Z (90) - Cannot use ENTER, R, or ESC keys as inputs. These keys can only be used by the evaluator during the automated testing phase. - The implementation of the test function will be responsible for the following: - Input: gameState object - Output: Return control key code or null to the input handling overriding the human control key inputs to play the game - You can return the same control key to mimic pressing down on a key, shifting between keys to perform action sequences such as jumping and moving right at the same time, and having delays between actions - The function implements the strategy for the respective test with reasonable logic based on the game description, controls, gameState, environment layout, and the game mechanics - The parameters and logic must be a reasonable implementation to test the desired aspect of the game following the strategy description to achieve the expected outcome - Track position history; if the agent stalls, inject random actions until progress resumes. - Automated testing controller structure: - Expose globally as window.game_testing_controller - Example implementation: ```javascript // implement in automated_testing_controller.js function getStickyKeysAction(gameState) { /* ... // returns sticky keys action */ } function getTestWinAction(gameState) { /* ... // code with logic to choose optimal actions to win the game */ } export function game_testing_controller(gameState) { switch (gameState.controlMode) { case "TEST_1": // enables the TEST_1 test function to play the game return getStickyKeysAction(gameState); case "TEST_2": // enables the TEST_2 test function to play the game return getTestWinAction(gameState); // Add other automated test cases here default: return getStickyKeysAction(gameState); // default to using sticky keys to play the game } } // Expose the game_testing_controller function globally window.game_testing_controller = game_testing_controller; // global hook export default game_testing_controller; // optional but convenient when used in other files ``` - UI Controls: - Add human/automated testing mode selection buttons - Button IDs: "test_1_ModeBtn", "test_2_ModeBtn", etc. - Update controlMode and button states on click, enabling the appropriate automated testing mode to play the game. Implement a fun and playable game based on the game concept input from the user. This 2D sandbox adventure game allows players to explore vast, procedurally generated worlds, mine resources, craft items, build structures, and engage in combat against various enemies and bosses. The game features a dynamic day/night cycle influencing enemy spawns, and progression is driven by acquiring better tools, armor, and weapons through crafting and defeating bosses, which in turn unlock new materials, NPCs, and world events. Game Name: Terraria Developer: 505 Games (US), Inc. Genre: Adventure # HTML REFERENCE TEMPLATE

{game_title}

// Add more test buttons with correct ID convention and click handlers

{game_description}

{game_controls}

 Output the code plan and game files in this format with NO OTHER TEXT: ... (Describe the game to the player, the objective, what they need to know to play the game. Do not mention the controls here. Keep it short and informative.) ... (Game controls as a list to specify the key bindings and the action they perform. Key: Action. Be specific about each key.) Write the automated testing plan: (write in 1-2 sentences "What are you testing and the intent of the test?") (write in 1-2 sentences "What is your gameplay strategy to test it?") (write in 1-2 sentences "What is the expected outcome? When do you consider the test successful?") // Add more tests (up to 7) For the javascript files: ... (code) HTML following the template (output last): ... (html code)
